problem:
Let A B C be an acute triangle with incentre I and A B 
eq A C. Let lines B I and C I intersect the circumcircle of A B C at P 
eq B and Q 
eq C, respectively. Consider points R and S such that A Q R B and A C S P are parallelograms (with A Q\|R B, A B\| Q R, A C \| S P, and A P \| C S ). Let T be the point of intersection of lines R B and S C. Prove that points R, S, T, and I are concyclic.
reference_solution: We will prove that \triangle B I R \sim \triangle C I S, since the statement then follows from \angle T R I=\angle B R I=\angle C S I= \angle T S I. Step 1. Let us prove \angle R B I=\angle S C I. We will use directed angles: \[ \begin{aligned} (B R, B I) & =(B R, A B)+(A B, B I)=(A Q, A B)+(B I, B C) \\ & =(C Q, B C)+(B I, B C)=(C I, C B)+(B I, B C), \end{aligned} \] which is symmetric in B, C. Therefore, analogously we would obtain the same expression for ( C S, C I ). Step 2. Let us prove B R / B I=C S / C I. Clearly B R=A Q and C S=A P. Angle chasing gives \angle I C B=\angle Q C B=\angle A P Q, and similarly \angle P Q A=\angle C B I, and so \triangle I B C \sim \triangle A Q P, from which the desired A Q / B I=A P / C I follows. This finishes the solution.
intermediate_generation: Use the reference theorem, reduce the goal, and finish with a direct tactic proof.
